Okay. So we're going to get down into finding clients now and talk about a lot of different techniques for doing it, things that I've done over the years, and other things that I haven't done I've seen other people do that have worked quite well. The first, you know, I'd like to start out by telling you about the best promotion I ever did. And I got a 100% response rate and it was this one. Actually, that's several promotions you're looking at there. The first one was I talked her into marrying me. The second one is I talked her into getting a Harley. That's actually your second one. But it really drives a point home. Now when I was pursuing Wendy, when I was pursuing Wendy, I didn't give her long lectures about how her life would be better by being with me. I didn't write an 18 page letter explaining what the men are from Mars, women from Venus, people said about marriage. I started out by saying, who's Wendy and what does she want? I knew I wanted her. What does she want? What did I have to do to get her, to help fulfill those desires? And so, you know, it's in many ways as we go through these slides, we're going to see parallels here between courtship and attracting clients. And with Wendy, I mean, you're going to think I'm an old softy or something, but I had a two story beach house and I had a banner made up custom that spanned the entire two floors that said, welcome home, Wendy, I love you. There was an airplane that went by carrying a banner that said on her birthday, that said happy birthday, Wendy, I love you. And so when she finally moved to C.S. de Quay where we lived at the time, we went out to breakfast one morning at this place, I went every single morning. This waitress comes over and says, I hope the hell you're Wendy. So, you know, it's really the courtship metaphor really works well when you're looking at the copy that you're writing as well. I mean, it's persuasion, right? And that's what courtship is. But anyway, this is the best sale I ever made. And now let's start out by looking at what makes a great client, okay? And I'm going to tell you a story that I'm not real happy with right now. It's making my life kind of miserable at work, but it's worth listening to, worth learning. The first thing is products that deliver what they promise. The worst thing in life that you can do is put strong marketing behind a weak product. Now, I have a client that has 300,000 opt-ins. These are names that we have permission to market to online. And he has some products that we're trying to sell to this list. And so last year, I did a really good promotion in February and sold about four and a half million dollars of this product to the list. Next month, I sold another three million. And we had a few months where we were kind of just barely making it. Then in July, we did six and a half million dollars. And in August, did three more. So it was really good for the client. He was happy. It was the best year in three years, I think it was. And the problem was that all of those products turned out to be lemons. They really kind of hurt the people that bought them. And of course, the people that bought them were his very best customers. Okay. So beginning in October, response to all promotions fell to pretty much zero. And no matter, now, now listen, think about this. If we had gone out with a half-ass promotion on any one of those products, we'd be in much better shape today if we had done a bad job selling those products. But when you have strong marketing and you get every prospect that even remotely could consider this product on board and then the product disappoints, you've poisoned the well. Right. And winning that trust back is very difficult if not impossible. You find yourself in a situation where you basically have to rebuild the client's list. So make sure the product you're asked to promote actually delivers what it promises. Don't be tempted to overpromise in your copy. Maybe it's a great product. Maybe it really does keep you from losing your hair. But if it doesn't make the hair grow back, then don't say it does. Right. Next, there needs to be a copy-centric culture at the company. They need to understand the primacy of copy. If they don't, you know, if they look at you as somebody that's just a commodity, as someone who produces words, as David says, then you're not going to be paid what you're worth. Worse than that, chances are promotions you write might not even get used if they're not aggressive at testing new copy and new promotions. So it needs to be a culture where when you say Claude Hopkins or you say Capels or you say Vick Schwab, person behind the desk nods his head. I understand. They need to have a sizable customer list. Now I just went through another one here. This client came to me and says, you know, we'd really like you to take over our marketing. And we have 400,000 email names. And we've never promoted to them. And I said, well, I could be good or bad. Okay. It could mean that these are virgin names just dying to buy something for you. Or it could mean that it's a bunch of people who are used to getting free stuff from you and really won't buy at First Blush. Then, after I agreed to help them with that admonition, you know, I'll be up front. If there's a risk, let them know what it is. You'll look like a hero if you overcome it. You won't look like a schmuck if you don't. So after, you know, working with them on it, decided to do an affiliate promotion with them. And out of that file of 400,000 names, I got four responses. All right. First thing I learned, they have 400,000 names, but only 70,000 of them have actually granted permission to be marketed to through email. So the list, right there, the list was much smaller. Secondly, they had never done the work with their ISPs. And so all of our emails went out, went straight in the spam folders, the spam folders. Third, the president of the company had never bonded with the names on that file. They knew what the company was, but they didn't know the person behind the company. And people don't bond with companies. People bond with people. So now I've gone back to that client and I said, you know, it was really good that we did this thing. We got no cost. We didn't get hurt, but we learned a lot of lessons. And now what needs to happen is at least six months of list building. We need to go back in the list. We need to bond with these people. We need to fix the ISP problems so we actually get delivery. And we need to find ways to get the rest of that 400,000 names to opt into marketing. Fortunately, these people do go to the website, so it's possible to do that with ads, banners, roadblocks, all kinds of stuff like that. So when you ask somebody how big their list is, if you're going to be doing online marketing, ask how many marketable names do you have, which I didn't do. And find out what they've done in the past that might bond their list to the person who will be signing the emails. Demonstrated list growth. If they're not growing their list, the names are older. The older they are, the less responsive they're going to be. So in direct mail, we have something called hotline names. These are the names of people who bought a product in the last 90 days. They're the most responsive part of any list. A lot of mailers, for example, will say, okay, you wrote me a new package, I really love this. Now what I'm going to do is I'm going to go to the list that typically worked best for me, but instead of mailing the whole list, I'm just going to mail the hotline names. And what that does is it reduces his risk of a loss because he's mailing to the very best names on the file. But he's already indexed those names, so he knows that if they respond at this level, the rest of the list will respond at some relative level. So, but look for demonstrated list growth. How many people are you adding to the file each month? The company should have an obsession with testing. They should have an insatiable curiosity. They should be kind of a pain in the butt for you because you'll go in and you'll write it one way and they'll say, you know, we really like this package, but we'd like to test a different headline. Or they'll come back to you after you get the control and they'll say, congratulations, you have the control that checks in the mail. Now, we want three more versions of this package immediately to test. And the test doesn't have to be copied, by the way. When Carly and I were working together on health and healing, we were doing just not health and healing, health resources, we were doing just direct mail. And we would do we would do our first mailing of the new creative. And the minute it became a control, we would then go straight back to the artist and we we would start out with a Magalogue. And we go straight back and we do a tabloid, a six by nine, a slim gym, you know, whatever format we could do because we got a winner. But what's going to happen? The second time, the same people see that promotion piece, response is going to notch way down. The third time, going to notch way down again. This way, we know the copy works, so now we can bicycle the formats. So that allows us to go back to the same people over and over and over again with a fresh looking package. And then when you throw in headline tests on top of that, you know, it really adds legs to a package and you can keep it out there making royalties for yourself much longer. The testing obsession really comes down to never being satisfied. You know, it doesn't matter how good this is responding. Like I told you, the health and healing package came back at 4% unheard of at the time to coal lists. And the very very next month, we were out there with different permutations, headlines and covers and stuff like that. Fail fast attitude. A lot of what you're going to be asked to do is going to be new customer acquisition or lead production promotions. And the key to making those work is to throw a lot of mud at the wall and see what sticks. That means what they want to do is they want to get short copy out there on a particular theme. This is just online stuff, okay? Short copy out there on a particular theme. Maybe it's four or five traffic drivers, maybe in the form of emails or whatever. A squeeze page and then that offers a free report or something and then the report itself which the client will usually produce. So then if that works, you know, and you're going to be paid a very small fee for this by the way, but if that because it's so little work really, but if it works, the client's going to come back to you and they're going to say, okay, look man, we really struck a vein on this one. What we want now is we want your very best work on more traffic drivers. We want five or six different permutations of the squeeze page. And now we're producing all of these leads, so now we need a silo or a funnel to convert those leads into customers. That's where the big money is. And that model's being used more and more. In fact, we're adopting it too at Weiss. Smart marketing managers, you can just tell sometimes, you know, you're talking to these people, you ask questions and there's this dead silence on the phone or they give you an answer that's makes it obvious to you that they're tap dancing. They don't really know the answer. You know, you need smart marketing managers, fair compensation structure. You don't have to hit it out of the park on every single one of these and get the very best deal available. You may want to, and we'll talk about this, do a first date with a client at a lower rate, understanding that it's like giving free heroin to children. The minute you hit a control with this guy, he's going to love you and he's going to coddle you and propose you and, you know, try to get as much of you as he can get. So it's okay to not go for every last dime on your first promotion with a client, but it does need to be fair and you need to be happy with it and client needs to be happy with it. Another thing that you can do is when you're talking to a client for the first time is ask who else is writing for him and then call those people and ask them does he pay on time? Is it pay fair? I've had clients withhold royalties because they said well we never agreed to pay you a royalty on list tests, you know, and it just, it drove me nuts. I stopped writing for that client as a matter of fact. He would, yeah. Can we hear that Dan Rosenthal says that a criteria is the client should be eight, should be something, can write big checks, you know, is willing to write big checks and is glad to do it. Yeah, he says, am I familiar with Rosenthal saying the client should be able to write big checks and happy to do it? Absolutely, that, you know, I've said basically the same thing. My main client pays me a hundred thousand dollar a month retainer, okay, not to write copy just a hundred thousand a month retainer and wires the money into my bank account and then tells anybody to listen including Bob Lye that, you know, how much he's paying me and giggles when I'm in the office and telling other people how much he's paying, you know. So yeah, they should be thrilled with it and, and yeah, and laugh when they're cutting the check. So, so those are the things that I look for when I'm, when I'm talking to a client. Where did I leave, there it is, when I'm looking at a new client. Now I've been burned on each one of these things at least once. Do you have a mic? Clayton, I was just going to say for these, for some of the folks in this room regarding our earlier conversation about finding a good mentor, I would put on this list a company with the terrific copy chief. Yes. In-house. That's where smart marketing managers come from. Yeah, that's one of the fastest ways to get a mentor even if you're a freelancer because they're going to shred your package over and over and ideally work with you to get it up to speed. How can you know that? Well, if you're going to work for Stansbury, you know, Mike Palmer is going to be at the helm, you're going to get an education and get paid for it. But there are other companies at the top of their fields and you can just look at their work output. You can look at what they're putting out there and say, this is good stuff, you know. As somebody who hires copywriters, I'm well aware of the fact that when I look at somebody's sample, I'm quite often not looking at their writing at all. I'm looking at their writing through the filter of a copy chief, you know. Yeah, so we're aware of that. But from your standpoint, you're really, you know, you're looking for that marketing manager. He's a copy chief, but he's also somebody who will come in there and come up with, um, nuts and bolts ideas to get the most out of your copy. And we'll talk a little bit about that tomorrow too. But like the idea, for example, I just gave you a word. As soon as we had a control, we had three more formats in the mail within weeks, you know. That's a smart marketing manager coming up with that, you know, if you see that. So, all right, so there's, you know, three words that I want to give you that got me probably my all-time breakthrough assignment. And, um, most of this you've probably heard, so I'll make it short. But I had moved to Prescott, Arizona, had $50. I had no money in my pocket. I moved from LA to Prescott, had no money in my pocket, had a wife and two kids, had a U-Haul truck with all my earthly possessions in it, unloaded the truck in Prescott, knew that I would be able to get grocery money only by returning the truck to U-Haul to get the deposit back, and it was in Phoenix. So, I drove down to Phoenix, 90-minute drive in the truck, got down there, um, uh, delivered the truck. My wife followed me down in the family car. And it just occurred to me, I hadn't planned this, that there was a company there called Research Publications. And there was a guy that ran it named Johnny Johnson. And he published Gary North, and these names don't mean anything to you probably, but Don McAlvaney and many others. And I'd seen the promotion he had for his newsletter, which was Daily News Digest, and frankly it was pretty bad, it was pretty weak. So, we passed a phone booth and I said, just on a whim, I said, hey, you know, pull over, I want to make a phone call. So, I just, you know, remember how the phone book was wired to the phone back in the day? And so, I just, I looked up Research Publications, and I called up and I said, Johnny Johnson, please, and they put me through, you know? And, uh, Johnny gets on the phone, and I said, Johnny, you don't know me, my name's Clayton Makepeace, and your sales copy sucks. I thought Johnny was going to bust a gut, he was laughing so hard, he thought it was hilarious. And so, we talked for a minute, and he said, well, that's a pretty precocious thing to say, you know? And I said, well, it does. I mean, I could beat your control, you know, I could double your response. And so, he goes, well, prove it. And I said, I'll tell you what, let's do this. I said, I'll write a promotion for you for zero dollars. If I beat your control, you owe me nothing. But if I don't beat it, if I do beat it, I want you to pay me sixteen hundred dollars. Well, this is where we've come from. This is part of your heritage. At that time, we were writing a full-on direct mail package for eight hundred bucks. No royalties. Okay, that was it. And we were doing it on a select, selectric typewriter. So, for every single draft you did, you had to retype the entire document, right? So, it was a lot more work and a lot less money. But anyway, sixteen hundred bucks was, you know, sixteen hundred bucks. So, he goes, deal. And so, I drove over there, met with him, got some materials from him, went home, wrote the package, beat his control by more than twice, and then ended up with the control on every newsletter he had. He had like six or seven letters at the time. So, being courageous, being a little bit outlandish, paid off really well for me. I talked about this a little bit of boot camp. I'm not going to spend a lot of time on it. But this is the importance of having a system. Now, Kelvin's got a system. And he won't tell us what it is. But it's working gangbusters for him. At some point, he sat down, he flowcharted the entire process. What needs to happen at each stage of this process to turn a cold lead into a paying customer, paying client. And he's got this thing all laid out and it's working gangbusters. And one of these days, he'll probably share it with us. Or we'll ban him from these things. But you need to do the same thing no matter how you're going to approach your client. Whether you've got an entirely web-based campaign, whether you integrate other media. Now, what I did back in the day when I was still beating the bushes for clients was I got a list of the companies I wanted to work for. I wrote a letter introducing myself. Much like the letter I described to you guys on the webinar the other day. And I said that next Monday, a FedEx truck is going to pull up outside your office and it's going to deliver the most important package you've received all year. And when you receive that package, please call me. And if I don't hear from you, I'll give you a ring on Wednesday. So as very matter of fact, NoHype didn't talk about how great I was or how much money I was going to make him or teaching the lessons I learned from Claude Hopkins or somebody. And so, you know, I'd send out, I think the first time I did, I sent out a hundred. And I got like three calls. And those conversations went well. Removing them down to the point of becoming customers. Then I started out calling to the people, just like I said I would, starting on Wednesday. Hey, did you get my package? Sorry about that. So, yeah, say did you get my package? I don't know why that's so funny, Carly. Did you get my package? And they would say, oh, no, or yes, whatever the answer was. And I said, well, you know, the thing is, I wanted you to see my work, but I've got a couple ideas for you. And then the conversation that I think will really help you. I didn't say, oh, please hire me. I don't have any food. I don't have any money. I have no clients. I really suck, but I want you to hire me. You know, I basically said, I have an idea that will help you. Did I have an idea? No, I didn't. Okay? But the next part of the process was, I was going to ask him enough questions so that I'd be able to make some suggestions to him. And so, and I think I go into more detail on this later, but the idea was, I had a system. I had a plan. I had a procedure. Okay? And I followed it religiously. And that's what got me beyond research publications out into the big world. And by the way, it got my name though. So make no mistake, your clients, your potential clients, they are incestuous as I'll get out. They go to, in the investment newsletter industry, we actually have something called the financial publishers round table. They get together once a year. You have to pay $1,500 to go, right? While you're there, you have to present your best marketing idea in the year, along with how it worked and the numbers and everything. And at the end of the thing, the person who presents the best idea gets all the money. Forbes is there, Motley Fool, Fortune, The Street, Jim Cramer's organization, all the Agora companies, boardrooms there. And everyone's, I'm trying to outdo each other, sharing with the group their best ideas. That's how incestuous they are. You talk about friendly competition. It really is that way. Plus, they meet on the phone once a month and they do the same kind of thing. So if you don't think, they talk copywriters, who's working for you? Who wrote that package I saw? Who wrote that webpage I saw? So they're always talking about that kind of stuff. And one of the best ways to get known, you send out letters, they'll be calling each other on the phone, well have you ever heard of this Carling Angley Gold person or this Clayton Makepeace person? And they'll be talking to each, no, but I saw some of the samples, they're pretty good, we haven't heard it yet. Well, we're thinking about it, and it'll start going, you'll start getting name recognition. By the time you get around to the people in week four, five, and six and call them, it's a really good chance they've heard your name and that can't hurt. So the idea was, I'd mail 100 letters, then I'd FedEx 100 care packages, and then I'd begin calling 20 prospects a day. This was my weekly plan. So I set aside one time during the day that I would cold call these people. And of course part of that is calling first and finding out who the decision maker is, who hires copywriters, and so you're connecting with the right person. So it worked really well, yes. Could we have a microphone? Just wondering your care package, was it all samples? Is that what it was? You know, I learned something from Gary Benavango along these lines. He had a one-page letter that just said, my name's Gary, and here's a list of my controls. And it was, there was no double spacing, and the list just filled the page. And then he had a little brochure that had some pictures, because there are people that'll say, well, I don't know what that is, but when you see it, you know, right? So then he'd had this montage of all of his controls, and it was so beautifully done. And it telegraphed, I'm not desperate. You can hire me, not hire me, I don't care. But these are my controls. And then it was like a three-fold eight and a half by 11 brochure, that was it. So very simple. Nowadays we have the internet, and we'll talk about that in a minute, but that can be a big part of whatever campaign you design. Now where we're going with all this is we're going to look at little pieces and components, things that you might do. So you, kind of a Chinese menu thing. So you guys can then, when we do our plan, you can pull some of this and pull some of that and decide for you and for your niche the best approach to developing new clients. Does that make sense? Okay. In your kit, you know, whether it's just a cover letter or whether it's a letter with a brochure, whether it's an email that links to your portfolio page, you know, whatever it is, there's several ideas here. And I have to say David was really helpful in one of these, the first one. Position yourself. Now if you're talking to a company and they start seeing you as somebody who's selling words, just somebody who can do the writing function, then you're not going to make any money with that company. You're just not. And that gets back to the primacy of copy idea. You know, they look at you as just somebody who could, you know, they might as well be paying a journalist to do this. If that's the way they look at copy, then you need to just say, well guys, look, if you ever get serious about this, give me a call. You know, I'm out of here. You need to position yourself, not just as a writer, but a strategic partner in growing this company. And this also leads on to where we're going to go next tomorrow at the end of the last session. But because it's part of making yourself indispensable, part of lifting yourself head and shoulders above your competition, everyone in this room is your competition. Okay, they can be your friendly competition, but at some point in time, you may come up against them on a copy test. Okay, so let's say the other person just is asleep right now and didn't hear this next part and you did. And you go in with something that differentiates you. You've got some additional training. Maybe you're a designer and a copywriter. Maybe you're a copywriter and a marketing consultant. Maybe you're a copywriter and a PLF, product launch formula expert like Paul. Maybe you're a copywriter with some management experience so you can help with personnel issues and so forth or processes in the company. At Phillips, there were a lot of great copywriters. Ben Zavango, Ratz, David, Paris, did you work for Phillips? Yeah, a lot of great copywriters. I'm the only one I know of that got paid $10,000 a month to just come fly up there once a month and sit in a room with Bob King for an hour. You know, it's a retainer. He valued me as a marketing advisor, marketing consultant, and I brought them Elaine Garzarelli and the Dolans and different editors to help expand their newsletter line. So, see, that was something that none of the other guys were doing and also helped with other product development ideas. So, you know, look for ways to lift yourself above the crowd. Be scarce. A lot of the letters that we, I looked at prior to the webinar we just had, would have a line that said, I can begin immediately. No, you can't. No, you're hard to get. You're valuable. You're in demand. People pursue you. They want you to work for them. There's no way you can begin immediately. Maybe you have an opening from somebody who canceled in two weeks, but no, you know. Now, if somebody calls up and says, I got a job and I need it done really fast, you can say, well, let me see if I can move my schedule around some. But you never say yes, you know, you never just accept it. You have to be scarce. You have to be perceived as being good and hard to get. Yeah, and charge a rush fee. Instead of going in and cutting your own throat by saying, please let me work for you. I'll even work for free or I'll work, well, I like free. But I'll cut my fees if you'll just hire me. You know, that smacks the desperation. And you think about it, okay, good copywriters are in demand. By definition, if you're not in demand, you're not a good copywriter. That's what the client's thinking. Now, we know it's not true, but that's what the client's thinking. So, you know, be perceived as good, be perceived as hard to get. This next one's also from David. He's obviously had some problems here that I haven't. But I think that might just be from my personality. But when I first started working with Weiss, I told them I don't do research. I don't do compliance. I don't do research. I write copy. That's what I do. And so they have never asked me to do my own research. They've never asked me to do anything related to compliance. I know I can write anything I want to. And if the compliance people have a problem with it, then they have to tell me. But there's no way I'm going to hold in my mind that I should say could instead of will or could instead of should, you know, when I'm making a claim in the copy, hold that in my mind while I'm trying to write the world's hottest copy on a particular product. So, you know, you guys take care of that. I will not do that. And whenever they ask you to do something extra, and we'll talk about this in contracts, whenever they ask you to do something extra, be thankful you have just done a negotiating point. You know? It's an opportunity to raise your feet like Marcella is going to do to me tomorrow. She's working on a very important launch. And it's turned out to be a lot more components than we thought it was going to be. So she's and rightly so. So, get the mic. Well, just because you've had a very long career in this, but you did do a lot of research, particularly on the health alternative health side of the market. You did a lot of your research. That was before wise. That was in the bad old days. Right. But I just want to make sure everyone knows that there are times where you will have to do your own research and it will benefit you, especially if the client is struggling to give you anything to work with. I'm using it really as an example of taking control of the process. The client is buying something when they buy you. You need to let them know what they're getting and manage your expectations. And we'll talk about in contracts, itemizing exactly what you expect from me. What are the components that I have to write? How many reviews are we going to have? What are the deadlines involved with this? You need to know those things. You need the client to know those things. Otherwise, I mean, I've had clients and I was so regretted ever accepting work from them because they paid me this $12,000 advance, but they were taking up an hour or two every single day with just meaningless, pointless meetings and stuff. And so you need to manage that process because that really cuts into your income. It cuts into your income stream. All right. So the next thing is to make a list of dream clients and get to know everything you can about them. We talked about the SRDS this morning. There are also industry directories. In the newsletter industry, there's the Oxbridge directory and it's like that thick and it's got every newsletter published in this country. So I started with that when I was beating the bushes for clients. You might be able to get an online copy now. It's very expensive, but you can go to a library and a good library will have a copy there that you can poach for free. This is, there are two kinds of industry directories. This is a third party, Oxbridge, just put this together. It's how they make money. And it lists the companies. It'll tell you the principles are. You can get a lot of good information. You can get phone numbers and all kinds of stuff out of there. But then there are directories that are put out by industry associations of their members, right? And you can get ahold of those as well. And you can go through there and also Dunn and Bradstreet. I don't know how crazy you want to get with this because a lot of these companies are going to be well known. You look at them, you say, oh, that's a big one. I recognize that company. But Dunn and Bradstreet, if you ever want to know what the revenues of a company are, how big it is, you can look at Dunn and Bradstreet. Also, SRDS, you can look at how big their list is. You can look at how many new names are adding to the list. And that'll give you a sense of how big and how successful the company is. And then, like we said earlier today, sign up, you know, buy some of their products, sign up to their free easing, you know, whatever, to get more information on the company. This is, for everyone in this room, this is going to be probably the most intimidating thing about this process. But at some point, you're going to have to talk to somebody, you know? And getting the energy on a Monday morning, it was easy for me. I had no money coming in, four miles to feed and rent payment to make, no cash in the bank. And if I didn't sell at least, I think back then it was a huge amount of like three grand a month or something like that in a copy, then somebody was going to go hungry or start bouncing checks, one, you know? So that was all the incentive I needed to learn to love the cold call. And the thing with cold calling is you need to, like I said, you need to have the plan. When you do get this person on the phone, you don't want to draw blank, I know I want to talk to you, what was it about? You got to have a plan for what you're going to say to the person. And the plan that, you know, that I followed was a pretty good one where you just basically say, I love your company, I love your advertising, I love your products, you know? I've got some ideas that I think could really help in the direct response area, you know? And I'd really like to talk to you about that. I know you're probably busy now, but could we set up a call for later in the week? Now if he says, no, let's talk about it now, you better have your list of questions, the ones we looked at earlier, ready to go, you know? And then at the end of the call, you just, you can just say, wow, this is good, look, I need to sleep on this, give me a couple of days, I'll get back to you with a proposal, you know? So, you know, it feels so natural, it's the way business is done, right? So then in a couple of days, he gets a letter, here's what I think you ought to test, here's what I think. And have some ideas that don't have to do with hiring you, you know? You know, have some ideas that, you know, I really think you should be offering this kind of special report, right? I really would like to see this in your marketing lineup or whatever, something that doesn't have to do with hiring you to write copy. So you start out the cold calling process by finding this person's, the decision maker's name and, and, and extension number and so forth. And then the second step is, you know, try to call early in the day executives, this is, this is one of those fun little things about B2B, right? Get around the gatekeeper. Executive assistants tend, tend to drag in slow in the morning and shoot out like a cannon at 430 or 5 o'clock sharp. The executive they work for is likely to be there long before in the morning and hang around for an hour or two later in the evening. So a really great time to get directly through to him would be at those times when the gatekeeper is missing. As you create your campaign, think about high visibility ways of delivering your messages, okay? FedEx is a great way. Western Union's a great way. Anything that says this is important, this is not the normal stuff you get during the day. You think about an $8, $12 FedEx package in, in light, you know, relative to what this guy could pay you. It's nothing, right? It's dropping the ocean. This is about your kit. We kind of jumped ahead to this earlier, but these are the things that you could put in a kit. Now, why have a kit instead of just sending an email and having them linked to a web page? The kit is three-dimensional. It makes you real. Makes you somebody. Anybody have, you know, you're not virtual anymore. You know, you're a real person. And this person can handle your letter, some of your samples, your brochure, maybe a CD-ROM that contains your samples. And it's going to get his attention a lot more than an email would get it. How many of you already have your own websites? Hmm. Quite a few. Okay, great. Well, for those of you who don't, it's pretty easy to do nowadays. This is what I did, and I came up with a whole bunch of, we have web designers to all our stuff, but Google, a website hosting company, visit the top five sites, look at them, look at what their deals are. Now, the way these things tend to work is they will charge a monthly fee for hosting your website. In return for you paying that fee, you're signing up for the long haul, right? You're giving them a credit card, and they'll just bang that card every month, okay, for hosting your site. In return for getting you to sign up for the long-term contract, they will have software on their site that allows you to create your website easy peasy, you know? And so you can upload your pictures, you can, you know, pictures of your samples, whatever you want, create your own website. They have templates, so it all looks really good and professional. So there's no reason for you not to have a website these days. And the hosting fees on some of these places are, you know, like, as low as four bucks, five bucks a month, so it's not a big expense. And it will come in handy down the road in a lot of ways. I mean, how could you use a website? Okay, well, one way would be to do videos and post videos on your website, instructional videos. Or when you have a winner, you know, you have a video of this, here's what I was thinking when I wrote this incredible winner that such and such a company is using to beat your brains out with, you know, competitively. So it could be instructional, right? You can have, of course, your own portfolio on there. You can also have an easy, it's real easy to do. And by the way, all of these places, once you sign up, you can go over to YouTube and watch all the videos you want on how to work this stuff. It's real easy. So you could add a blog, you could add a bulletin board, you could, you know, you can pretty much do whatever you want to engage writers. Now, we have a real simple website for Response Inc. That hasn't been updated in forever and probably needs to be. But when people Google my name or Google Response Inc., which is our company name, they're going to find this link. It'll be served up on the first page of search results. You go there and you should just go through the process. It's not a real fancy site, real simple. Like I said, it hasn't been updated in like six years. But there's a form on that site that says, I want to work with Clayton. And then it asks them to answer a whole bunch of questions about their company. Now, I want to know what's your product? What's the link to your website? How many of these have you sold? How many employees you have? What are your annual revenues? You know, there's critical questions that I need answers to before I'll even talk to these people. Right? So then, now, you may be a lot less picky than me. So, you know, you may not care in the same way, but Dagaana really is good to know when you're talking to somebody to already have some of this information. And to, you know, this also allows you to go out and find the, find additional research and learn more about their stuff. This next tip, I don't think it's a good idea, but I included it just because somebody else did. That was researching. The jobs boards. This is where you become kind of a commodity, you know? In some instances, you may go, well, you may go to Warrior Forum or you may go to one of the, one of these jobs boards and find yourself in a situation where a client is just a perfect fit for you, you know. You've done something in this genre. You've had a success in this genre. You have a spec assignment that you've done in this genre that you're proud of. In a situation like that, you come in with some credibility and you can negotiate a decent fee for yourself. But to me, these kinds of things tend to be more really survival kind of situations. They're not the kind of situations where you're typically going to be able to negotiate a large royalty and so forth. But it is an opportunity and it sure doesn't hurt to have yourself listed there. You can also get some real samples that you sent in the mail. So, the folks I've talked to have done that, have done it so that they could have a real portfolio to show people. They weren't going to do it forever, but it led them to get some real pieces. Yeah. There's a lot of writers doing this and the ones that I've seen doing it, a lot of them are just journalistic writers and not really direct response people. But I don't know. Could work for you. Social media, we don't have a lot of followers yet on Facebook, but we started kind of building a Facebook presence a few months ago. We have like 10,000 followers, I think. The good thing about it is that, okay, I was talking to Perry Marshall. Perry Marshall wrote the Bible on paper click. Perry Marshall says when I asked him, I need help with paper click with Google AdWords advertising. And which is specialty and he says, well, yeah, I'll give you some names. But he says, tell you what, I hate to admit this, you know, because, you know, I wrote the book on this, but I don't even use paper click anymore. He says, Facebook has gotten so good. So, with a Facebook dashboard, you can go in there and you can identify exactly who's going to see your ad. And much more effective, much more cost effective. And so that's the one side of Facebook. The other side of Facebook for you guys. Now, what does that mean? That means you could identify particular kinds of companies and get messages to them on Facebook and bring them to your Facebook page and get them to reply in some way. That's way one. Way two is that you have on your Facebook page constant stream of valuable information that makes people like that page so that you show up on their timeline or on their, what is it, newsfeed. Right? And so, you know, the funnest thing to go with is stuff like memes. Do you know what memes are? And it's just a photograph where somebody has put some funny or wise or, you know, whatever saying on there that people will want to share with other people. You know, we all want people to think we're clever. Well, virtual cleverness is now passing these memes around as if you created it. You know, people go, oh, that's so funny. But, you know, you didn't really create it. So, you know. But you put something up there that is controversial or that brings value to companies and executives' life. They'll like your page, which will get you more likes, more likes, more likes, you know. So, it's a great way to go. You can put videos up, like we're talking about on your own website. You can put instructional videos up. You can have links to special reports you've written on various aspects of copywriting to demonstrate your expertise. You can ask your clients to post on your Facebook page, you know. You won't always be dealing with the top guy at these companies. And you can ask a marketing manager or somebody you're working with, would you go to my Facebook page and say something about that promotion we just did or did really well. You know. So, Facebook marketing is really powerful. So is YouTube. Get your own channel and start posting videos up there. I was with Joe Polish a year ago or so. And Rich Sheffrin, and we were standing in front of the hotel there in Delray. And Joe just pulled out this little high definition video camera and held it up like this. We had this selfie. But it was the three of us talking about copier marketing. And he penned over to himself, well, Clayton, what do you think about this? And he penned over to me, you know. And it's been up on Facebook for two years now, you know. And people comment on it all the time. If you search for me on Facebook, you'll find a bunch of corny stuff on there about me. But it's a great way to, you know, to get your name known and to gain credibility in the industry. So, you know, interviews with clients, you know. Video, it could be text or a video or a podcast. Share copywriting secrets. You can go into cables. How easy it is to create content for something like this. Go into cables, I showed you the book, and get and create some checklists based on his, yeah, you'll see him in the book. And just, he'll have all of these checklists kind of, take those checklists and just go ahead and give them credit and say, I love this checklist by John Capels. Bang. You know, for making sure your copy sings. And great content. As long as you give credit where credit is due, there's nothing wrong with that. Lessons learned. You know, one of the things that people love the most is when I tell them when I got spanked. You know, when I, when I have a big flop, you know, and I'll say, man, was I stupid on that one. And here's what I did wrong. Here's where I went wrong, you know. And people love it. Clients especially love it. You know, they'll say, wow, I'm glad he made that mistake with somebody else and not me. You know. So, but, but it also lets them know that you're somebody who's evolving, who's learning and, and who values these lessons. You know, the times you flop are far more valuable in terms of your education, your ongoing education, the copywriter than the times that you're a success. The times you're a success, by the way, are the most dangerous times you're going to have as a copywriter. Because then you start getting cocky. And then you go from cocky to sloppy. You know. And all of a sudden you're putting copy out there that, where you haven't gone over it with a fine tooth comb, and you've missed some of the points that can really make it strong. Get a mic. I really think that's so huge. You know, I have learned much more from my, my bombs, not bombs, or things that didn't work. But you've got to go back and you've got to look at it and go, what did I do that this didn't work? And really keep going backwards. Was it, what's in the copy that didn't work? What was it in my thinking when I started out? At what point did I go wrong? And now there's like 20 things that I know not to do when I start a package, because those 20 things led to, you know, led to bombs in the past. Yep. Yep. We call them post mortems. And what we like to do is we'll run a campaign. Now, stuff online is very different. I, I miss direct mail so much, where you just wrote one direct mail package, just had back and collected royalties, you know. Online is just like endless, you know. And the campaigns go on for days, weeks, months. And so there are all of these different components. So we, at the end of a campaign, it may be a month long or two months long, we'll go back and we'll look at every subject line on every email. We look at every sender on every email, because I believe in many cases a sender can be as important as a subject line. And we'll look at the sales page and we'll dissect it. Where do we, we'll look at the offer, where do we go wrong there? How could we have made that better? And we get all the metrics, so we know the click-throughs, we know the open rates, we know the, you know, the conversion rates and so forth. And three of us will sit there and just crunch every number and try to learn a lesson from this, so that next month we'll put a better campaign out there. So when we had, we had a rough patch with health resources, where we had prepared like five megalogs and we put them in the mail and all five of them died. I mean, just crashed and burned, right? And I said, and we found out on a Friday, I said, Wendy, cancel your weekend plans, we're going in the office, we got to figure out what we did wrong. And we're sitting there and we're going over the copy and the graphics and it looks good and I can't really find anything wrong with it. And then Wendy said something like, well, they dropped this in the middle of Katrina, you know? Now, I was starting to doubt my value as a copywriter, but half of my mail was stuck in a, in a sectional center down in Arkansas or Louisiana or someplace, you know, because it could, yeah, and ruined, yeah, in the flood. And so that, you know, by going through that process, instead of just saying, well, it didn't work, by going through that process, we retested those packages and every one of them worked. Could you? Okay. Clayton about, should that be a question that even when you're looking at working with a client, is to even ask, what do you do after mailing or your campaign or whatever it might be? Because I think, I know I've worked with people where we've, whether it won or lost or whatever, never done that. Right. And so that seems like that might be something that's really important. It's a hard call to get. When someone calls up and says, well, you know, we got the results, you bombed, right? My response always was, why? You know, what do you think, asking the client? Phillips used to play these little games that us copywriters weren't supposed to know about, but they would launch a new newsletter and they'd say, we have chosen you to launch this new newsletter and you'd be, oh, thank you, you know, and you would have your, launch me, you'd write the copy, it would go out. And only at some point down the road, do you find out that they also hired Gary Benzavenga and Jim Ratz and two or three other people and were testing all three of you against each other. Now, if they had told any of us in advance, and Gary has said this and so has Jim, you know, that if they ever found out that they were in a three-way split with Clayton, they would decline the job. And I sure as hell would too, you know. So, but that call, when you get the call, you go in there and you say, help me, I have to understand this. The marketing manager has the advantage of living with that product. He has the advantage of having seen a lot of promotions of that product, the testing of a lot of themes of that product. And so he'll be able to give you some really good feedback on what he thinks went wrong. And I can tell you nine times out of ten, you know, if you've done your job, it won't be anything about your copy. It'll be that the market change. Tell you what, O.J. Simpson killed Nicole. We couldn't get a heartbeat out of the market during that entire trial. Ronald Reagan got elected president. Response crashed. We had elected the Messiah. Investors didn't need help anymore. They were just going to put their money in the market and collect dividends and capital gains. There are certain events in the news that will kill response. Yes, Wendy. The reverse of that was the crash in 2008 and 2009. You know, that became a windfall. Yeah. Temporarily. Yeah, because all of a sudden, you know, everyone's a genius in a rising market, right? But when stocks crash, all of a sudden, people need advice. Chris has another question. You're even talking with your client, like, or the potential client, even ask them, how do you do postmortems on your campaigns? Yeah, you can ask them if they do. Frankly, a lot of companies don't. Yeah, it's been my experience. They'll just move on. And you know, if you're the one that walks in and says, let's do a postmortem, you know, let's tear this apart, you get brownie points for that, you know, because the managers know they should be doing that, you know, otherwise, they're going to keep doing the same thing over and over again, expect better results. That said, don't rely on the marketing manager to do it. You've got to be able to do that. Right. I mean, you've got to be able to do that. You've got to be able to look and know. And it's easier in hindsight, knowing it bomb than it is before you do it, or else tell it, sell it, show it to your friends. Yeah. And let them tell you why. You know, the thing is that the marketing manager adds a different perspective is all I'm saying. You should definitely do it yourself. But asking the marketing manager, because he has that history with the product and with his market, he'll be able to add some perspective. Oh, we have another one up here, microphone. Over here. How likely is it if a company comes to you to write something that they've also hired other more junior or even more senior copywriters to write the same thing? And if you ask, would they tell you? Most of them will either tell you or Hem and Haw, which is telling you. Right. And is it something they would normally do or just some things? Some companies do. Now, Phillips was the best marketing company I have ever worked for. And that includes the marketing companies that I built from the ground up. Phillips was amazing. I mean, they had their faults, but they were very aggressive marketers. And there were a couple of times, I mean, I finally had to get to the point where I had a contract that said they could not put another writer up against me. I could go and try to knock off anybody's control, but they were contractually bound not to put another writer up against me until I got the control. If I got the control, then I was fair game. But at that point, too, I would go in and I would write new controls, because I wanted to maintain that royalty. So could I assume that they might be having someone else do something that they came to me to write? I don't know how many companies are doing that now. I don't think that many. I haven't seen it lately, but I do know Phillips was doing it. Thank you. And when I caught them, there were red faces all around. It was actually kind of funny. It would depend on how much money the company has, too. A lot of money, print, is very expensive these days. So the chances of them hiring two or three top-level copywriters and putting them up against you are very important. Wendy's making the point that direct mail is very expensive these days, and so are copywriters. And so hiring two or three top-level guys to do this would probably be a mistake, or at least so costly that they probably won't do it. So all right, I don't think you know, you may be going for a while on this. Okay, this is a good one. Just keep this principle in your mind. Okay? On the web, content is king. If somebody has a website about a particular topic, the one thing that they're starving for is content on that topic. So if there's a content about direct marketing or about direct response or even for health companies, for example, whoever has that website would be very happy to get articles, any kind of content at all from you. And so you write an article about a package you wrote or some technique you tested or something like that. You get that on the web. You can also look at the Direct Marketing Association and other associations and industry groups and offer to provide content. Write an article. Here's something that I thought of. Here's something that worked for me. Here's an idea that I had, and write an article, make it fun, make it interesting, and offer it with a link to your website. Okay? So-and-so as a copywriter, specializing in health promotions. Click here for more information. You get, you, chances are, you know, and I've had websites who don't know me from Adam, but somebody told them I was a financial copywriter or a health copywriter and they've actually called me and asked for this. So it can be really effective. By the way, it's called Content Marketing and it's a very effective way to drive traffic. And we, monthly-no, Zax, Investment Research, writes 4,000 articles a month. 4,000 articles a month. They have a staff of writers who do nothing all day but sit there on Google and search for what people are looking for on Google. What investment topics are people searching for, right? Then they write a five-our-five paragraph article. Let's say people are looking for information on Apple today because it's in the headline. Somebody will sit there and write up a little five-paragraph article on Apple using the word Apple. Apple, Apple, you know, two or three times in each sentence or whatever, so the spiders, the search engines can find it and they get that out there. And they're cranking out five, each writer, five, ten articles an hour or more. And they have this whole staff of people doing nothing but. Each article goes on their website to draw organic traffic to the website. It's also syndicated through other people like MarketWatch and Yahoo Finance and so forth. It's called content marketing. What you're doing here is kind of the same thing, but it's just for you. You're getting your name out there and you're getting links out there. Yes. The question that I have for you, Clayton, which may be apropos for many, is that if you are doing content marketing for people's websites or companies and they're very happy with your content marketing, how do you get them to recognize that you want to shift over to the copywriting side or that they should consider your copywriting in addition to the content marketing? Since sometimes these companies have different marketing people associated with each one. Sure. There's no real answer for that because every company is different. But what I found is if you establish yourself as a success in one area and you say, you know, I'd really like to take a stab at this over here, can I just one time, let me try it. I think I can beat what you're doing now. A lot of companies will do it. They'll just go for it. The problem comes in when the content marketing is being managed by the editorial department and the direct response copy is being managed by the marketing department. But you can still bridge that gap just by asking the editorial people to introduce you to the marketing people and sometimes they'll do it quite often, I would suppose. So we're going to have to kind of move along here if we can. Offer to speak at marketing conferences, same idea. I've given you some contact information there. The DMA is dying for people to speak and they contact me all the time asking me, dude, I've never done it. But Craig Huey built Infomat on this kind of stuff. There's a hilarious story about that. I'll tell you when we have more time. But yeah, he built his whole company on this. He'd show up at this place, act like an expert, next thing you know, he had a bunch of clients. Write an e-book. Rich Sheffrin is the past master of this. His little secret was that in the title of your e-book you would have a word that is very seldom used. So he came up with the idea of the Internet Manifesto. His word was manifesto. And so, and then he wrote this e-book and it was search engine optimized and which just basically means it. He had identified a certain number of keywords and he repeated them over and over and over again in the text. And he brought in like 60,000 people who downloaded that free e-book and by doing so they all opted in. He got their email address and so forth. Now that's a B to C kind of approach. But you can do the same thing, your B to the B of your client by doing, demonstrating your expertise in an e-book, making it available online to potential clients. Cosie up to Lori or the list broker that Carline was talking about or a printer or another copywriter. I think that I just blew a slide that we're about to see. But obviously for the obvious reasons. This I think was Marcella's idea. I think it's a really good one. Was it? Okay, short pay, long pay. So every day you're working, working, working, doing everything you can to bring in every dollar that you can. So you've got a certain fee structure and these are fairly short-term turnaround jobs. At the same time, whenever you can, try to have a longer-term job in the hopper where you're taking a much bigger chunk of the money. So you're doing the, let's pick a number, say $12,500 advance against a royalty of 10% of sales. And you're doing that and that's your standard fee, standard fee. Well, every once in a while and look for these opportunities. Like I did with the guys from Brussels. A longer-term thing, it's going to take them six months to design this ultimate survival course thing. And it's going to take, I won't see any money from that for probably nine months, is my guess. But it's worth it. It's in there, it's working and at the nine months, it has the potential to bring in millions of dollars. So it's like diversifying your portfolio. If you're an investor. Same basic idea. Okay? I get excited when I hear people like David talk about writing an eight-page letter that brings in millions of dollars. That's really old, hardline, direct marketing. That's what we do. That's what you have the capacity to do that. So from time to time, you're going to see the client who has no idea what he has in terms of product. He has lousy marketing and you're going to look at him and you're going to go, oh my goodness. If I could just write a promotion for this guy, I'd do it for free, but I want 30% of the money. So in hypothetical situation, he's got 100,000 names on his list. You do this promotion, it goes out, it brings in a million dollars. And it's real easy with the next one because we've talked about it several times this morning. When you approach a client, have an idea or at least the plan to generate an idea after having him answer your questions. I told you yesterday, it feels like yesterday already. The guys in Brussels, I have a $3,000 an hour consultation fee on my website. They contacted me perfectly willing to pay it. And I basically said, no, we'll have one hour together. If I think I can help you, then I'll charge you the fee. And basically, what did that do? That established the value of the information I was going to give them at being worth $3,000 an hour. So pretty much anything I suggested after that point sounded like a screaming bargain because if I'm offering now to write a complete promotion for you, that's going to take two weeks, figure that out at $3,000 an hour. So have a fee that you're going to charge for consultation and be willing to discount it or just completely get rid of it if you see an opportunity with a client. Now the Brussels guys are long money. We're just talking about short money, long money. The Brussels guys were long money. So it was worth me walking away from that fee. It would have been 10 or 12,000. I'm hoping to come out of this between Paul and me to come out of this with $300,000, $400,000. But it's a great way to establish your value. Ambush them. This is another take on the idea of doing spec assignments. It's slightly different though. Instead of waiting for someone at AWAI or me or someone to say, hey, do a spec assignment on XYZ, you go into your niche, you go online, you pick your dream client, you pick a product that he's promoting now, you study the promotion that he's using to promote it, and then you beat it. You just beat it. You write the bet. This is a beautiful way to do it. There's no deadline. There's no schedule. You can take as long as you want. The guy that has the control was performing on a schedule. You have all the time in the world to beat that promotion. Only when you think you have something, and again, show it to your mastermind group, show it to your friends, read it to your spouse, make sure that you are at the point where you're totally confident of it, then at that point, present it to the company and say, hey, I wrote this for you. All I'm asking is that you test it. Now, if it's an online promotion, this kind of thing should work really well because online doesn't cost the client anything. If you write a direct mail piece, he's going to have to pay 700 bucks a thousand to test the dog on thing. So writing an online promotion for the product is a way to go. A little side effect, slightly devious, but you can then have that in your portfolio and say, well, here's a piece I wrote for Agora, or I wrote for Stansbury or so forth. It's true. You did. Offer a free critique. Craig Huey built his business on this kind of thing. And the funny story there real quick is we did our health and healing launch, 4%, blowing it out, three new shifts of people just count the money, DMA conference comes up, Craig Huey stands up front, holds up the mailing piece and said, this is the worst direct mail I have ever seen in my life and proceeded to tear it to shreds. All the Phillips girls are in the front row. They know, and of course they've been told, you are not to let anyone know how good this is because we want to get probably a full year with no competition in this niche. It was the world's first alternative health newsletter. So we don't want anyone else, Gore, or anyone else, figuring out how profitable this niche is until someone wakes up and says, how come I have a hundred of these in my inbox? So Craig Huey helped us with that. He had informed everybody that this was a piece of crap and don't ever do it like this. And of course, made himself look like a total boob in the process. But you can do that. They're looking for content. You can go to the conference. You can stand up and you can say, I'm going to critique some mail packages and show you what's working in a particular niche and what isn't. Work for free. This is basically the idea of saying, look, I don't even want any money up front. I just want a royalty. Let me write this. I think I can be what you have now. I just want 10% of the money. That's where you pay a shoe salesman. I'll take 10% of the money. This works especially well when you're dealing with people who don't know what they have or have lousy marketing right now. Join a team or create your own. We've talked about this a lot today. What David does, what Paris does, what Marcella does, they have their teams that they've created. Joining a team like that, if they have openings, and I don't think they do right now, it's like every writer's dream. But if you can't get on that team, make friends here and create your own team. How does that help you get clients? You create a team with three other people that can't get clients either. The way it helps you get clients is that those people will. You've just quadrupled your chances of getting a part in what could be a very successful package as a team effort. You've quadrupled your chances of getting that assignment that you can do. When I first got started, when I first, okay, that Craig Huey guy that was such a boob, he was the guy that gave me my first job as a direct response copywriter. And when he gave me the job, he told me, look, if you can sell a million dollars worth of creative in the next 12 months, I'll give you a $20,000 bonus. So over the next 12 months, I sold over a million dollars of creative. Around Christmas time, I go in and say, need my bonus. Here's the spreadsheet. I did it. He fired me on the spot. Fired me on the spot, the slime ball. So I went out, I said, okay, fine, not a problem. None of your clients that I brought you even know your name. So as of right now, you have no clients. I'm taking them all with me. And it was great. I made $35,000 my first month on my own. It was two years income at the time. So I was going somewhere else with that too. Yeah. Oh, thank you. So, all right. So then, I'm living in LA, right? And the clients that I was working with, really good for a couple of months, but then started tail, we did their work and starting to tail off. We weren't getting royalties anyway. So my buddy and I that also got fired at the same time created something called copy overload. And the theme was only a ski mask and a loaded 45 will get you more money faster than a package from copy overload. And we had a picture of guy in a ski mask with a gun, right? And then it just basically said, because even copywriters need a vacation. And so we went around to all the big name, big five ad agencies in LA, B, B, D and O, all of the big guys. And we sent these things out to them. And we said, look, there are lots of times when you got more copy than you can produce, or you could take on more opportunities if you could just get the copy done. That's what we're for. And we're working out of our bedrooms, but we were copy overload. We're great. We got all kinds of great assignments. And I tell you what looks good in your portfolio. You show somebody that you just did a package for Bob Stone, or for B, B, D and O, and or for the end client, some major bank. And all of a sudden, man, you can get a lot more jobs. Instant credibility. All right. Well, this doesn't require a lot of time, but the basic idea is why do brain surgeons get paid more than general practice doctors, right? I'm a copywriter is okay. Lots of copywriters. You can go on Yahoo and find out that the average salary of a copywriter is about $80,000. Okay? So why in the world do anybody pay you more? Well, I'm not a copywriter. You know, I'm a specialist in health, or, you know, selling health products. That's my specialty. And I'm good at it, you know? So be sure to specialize. Don't try to be everything to everybody. Have a specialty. I love this picture. Create Scuttlebutt. Again, whenever you have a success, let it be known. Whether it's personal phone calls to the guy's competition, stuff on your website, whatever. All right? Get it out there. Our industries are incestuous. They will talk to each other about you. Get an agent. Kevin Finn is John Finn's son. You were talking about John Finn. It's funny how our roads, our paths crossed. When I got that first job as a copywriter, it was with InfoMap, which was a company that John Finn had just sold to Craig Huey. So the first person I met in Southern California was John Finn, and he was in his offices over at King Harbor. He had a condo there. And John passed when was it last year. And his son is now doing the same work. And I've used him. I've hired five or six copywriters through Kevin. And submit your samples. Talk to him. Maybe he'll take you on. John Finn brought us Arthur Johnson, one of the greatest copywriters of all time. It was John that found him out. We talked about this. No need to go into detail. I think this was Carleen, who was one of our experts here, just decided, you know, I've got like three people I've written for across the country. Was this you? Got in a car. Got in a car. Did you throw your kids in the back seat or something? Yeah. Took a couple of kids with her. Just goes to client A, sits down. Most time we don't even meet our clients. It's all done over the phone and so forth. Microphone. Oh, I'm going to go ahead and take a picture. What's the experience of taking that road trip? I did it for two years in a row and I more than doubled my income just doing that. And it was, well, actually it wasn't just a road trip. I actually flew. I had one client in California. I went, I hadn't met them. I had written a package with them. It was doing really well. But I didn't know them. And it was all, my theme that year was just get to know your best clients and face to face and everything. So when we, Milan, I took her with me actually on that, on that trip and we went there. I just, they said, well, so what's the purpose of the trip? I'm like, I just want to take you guys out to lunch. That's all. You know, I didn't, I wasn't trying, it was nothing but just saying, I want to get to know you. I want to, I want to face, you know, so when I talk with you, I know who you are. And that was it. Took them out to lunch. While we're talking to lunch, they start saying, oh, this is so wonderful. But we have to get back soon because we have so much work to plan for the next year because we have, we have these new products coming out and we need to get the writers for, hey, are you available to write some packages for us, you know? And I booked two packages over lunch, you know, that I would not have even known about unless it was. I think Paris did something like this too. And I seem to remember the story of laying out all their premiums on the table, you know, and then, and seeing a button, here he comes. He'll tell you about it. And then I'll tell you my favorite Carline story about premiums. I did it, you know, everything I've done, basically, I just copy what Clayton's doing. So there's no mystery, no magic. So Clayton says become more than a copywriter, be a trusted advisor, consultant, an integral part of their business, a partner. So how do you do that? Well, it's risk reversal, right? Make it brain dead simple for them. So I called a client who I'd written a number of really big successes for and I said, hey, I'm going to be in your town next month. What if I stop by and we just, you know, shoot the shit or whatever. And so we get there and we just laid out all their stuff all over the table, all the inserts they were doing, you know, the ride-alongs that come with the newsletter. And on the spot, I was just giving them headline ideas that they later tested and got lifts from. And then we did other things. And gradually, it morphed into this thing where I was advising them on everything. I mean, not just copywriting, but marketing strategy, business strategy, hiring people. Was this SoundView? Yeah. Yeah. And so, you know, I'll talk about a little bit more on my talk tomorrow, but I became like a partner in the company and everything went through me and I got a piece of everything. Yeah, I got a call from him. He says, you've been doing this for a while. How do I structure the deal? And so we kind of talked about it a little bit. Next thing I know, he was doing it. It's fantastic. So road trip, you know, you'll get your first jobs, you know, over the internet, over the phone, whatever. But at some point, showing up at a very promising client's office and just basically having a brainstorming session at that point, you lay out all those direct mail pieces or you all get on a computer and you look at his websites or whatever. And as you're doing it, it's just a lot of this, what we do is common sense. But you're looking at what he's doing with a fresh set of eyeballs, you know. And so you look at his stuff and you'll ask questions. Again, don't try to be the big shot and say, oh, if you only did it this way, you'd make it. And we try that it failed. So don't do it that way, but just ask questions. Have you tried this? Have you tried that? What if you did this? What if you did that? Okay. And you're going to hit on some that really work. And then, you know, chances are the client says, well, would you like to write it? You know? And so you just got a new assignment. So I think everyone in this room should do this no matter what. Okay. And including me. Okay. You should start your own company. Start a web-based company. I don't care if you're selling handbags or, you know, vitamins, which isn't that hard to do, right? You can get a formulator and start a vitamin company. I don't care what it is. Even if you're reselling somebody else's product, go through the process yourself to two great rewards. Number one, you might be successful and sell your website to Facebook for a billion dollars. Secondly, or at least have a nice supplemental income from it. And think about that for a minute. Okay. You guys are copywriters. You identify yourself as copywriters. Most of you identify yourself as freelancers. How much money do you need a month to retire? I mean, just need every month to retire. Five grand, ten grand, something like that. Okay. How hard would it be to create a company that generates five or ten thousand dollars for you? An online-based company. Okay. So that's number one. It's your retirement plan. Okay. Anything that you earn over and above that is gravy, right? Secondly, in order to do this, you're going to have to learn lead production, pay per click, Facebook advertising. You're going to have to learn how to do these things. Fortunately, they're not that hard. You can learn by doing their videos on them. There are seminars on how to do it. And you can do these things. Well, now what's happened? Well, instead of just a copywriter, you're a Facebook guy. You're a PPC guy. You're a social media guy. You're a content marketing guy. Because you learn to do it on your own site. You train yourself to do it. So not only have you created a retirement income for yourself, and believe me, ten grand a month isn't that hard to generate with a website of your own. Okay. But you've learned all of those things, and now you're a multi-talented person. They're not just hiring a copywriter that they're used to paying X for. They're hiring somebody who can be a strategic partner in growing the company. Right? Denise isn't here. I was going to ask her the specifics on this. I don't know what they are. But the new jobs board that they put up there gives you a place on the web. There can be a link to your website. If you have your own website with all your own samples on it. And you can write the copy on your page and describe yourself and what you do and so forth. Okay. All right. Do not, when you send a letter to a client, attach a $1 bill to the top of the letter. Okay. Client knows anything about direct response. He'll know you stole that idea from Gary Halbert. And probably about a billion copywriters after Gary. Don't send a gift. Now, I really loved all of the presents that I get. I get stuff, Harley Davidson teddy bears. I get a model of my Porsche car. I, you know, got all of these presents. It's never going to sway me to hire a copywriter. When I hire a copywriter, the only thing I care about is can that person produce? Can they make me look like a hero to a client? You know? So don't waste your money. It's not going to help. We're going to read what you send us anyway. You don't have to bribe us to read it in that way. Our bribe is, man, if we find the next Paris Lampropolis or the next Marseille or the next Carline, they're going to make us a lot of money. Don't say they laughed when I sat down to write copy. As derivative. They just says, hey, I know how to plagiarize. You know, hire me. So don't show off your vocabulary. Don't, you know, don't speak in $10 words. Keep it simple. Part of impressing a potential client, this gets back to what we said on the webinar. Part of impressing a potential client is speaking, understanding the context in which you're speaking. So if you're talking to a guy who's an expert mail manager or a direct marketing manager, you're talking to somebody who's pretty smart, right? And so very simple words, very simple idea. One idea, no conflict, no lack of clarity of vision. Don't use power words. Now, to my everlasting chagrin, we published a book called Power Words. And it was something that I think Anthony put together. Emotional trigger words. Yeah. And so I get a lot of letters from copywriters now who basically have written me a letter and then gone through that letter and put every single one of those words in there. So they're not going to lift my response. They're going to explode my response, you know. So, you know, don't bother with that. You know, respect your prospects intelligence, right? And don't over promise. Like I said earlier, I like the low-ballot. I like the, you know, lower expectations and let people know that what we do is hard, you know? There are no guarantees. You can write the best package in the world. And if another airplane goes down off of China and the news is filled with that and people are obsessing over it, your package is going to flop, you know? So, okay. So that gets us to the end of the presentation. We were supposed to finish last hour.
